sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 38, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 37 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the colors on the board: O (orange), S (sky blue), W (white), Y (yellow), B (black).
- S: sky blue squares (the two 5x5 movable squares)
- B: black walls
- Y: yellow background (left side)
- O: orange background (right side)
- W: white (small objects at edges, likely HUD elements)
There are no special target markers. The goal must be related to the positions of the two squares relative to each other or to the maze structure.
Let me think about this more carefully. The maze has:
- A left chamber (yellow background) and a right chamber (orange background).
- The left square is in the left chamber, and the right square is in the right chamber.
- The central corridor at cols 29-33 separates the two chambers.
Looking at the transition history, I notice that when the squares are at the same row range (e.g., both at rows 49-53), they can both move UP/DOWN together. But when they're at different row ranges (e.g., left at 39-43, right at 44-48), they move independently.
The key insight from the transitions is:
- LEFT moves left square LEFT and right square RIGHT (away from each other)
- RIGHT moves left square RIGHT and right square LEFT (toward each other)
So the squares move in opposite horizontal directions. This suggests the goal might be to:
1. Bring both squares to the same column position (using RIGHT to move them toward each other).
2. Bring both squares to the same row position (using UP/DOWN).
3. Have both squares meet at the center.
Currently:
- Left square: rows 39-43, cols 14-18
- Right square: rows 44-48, cols 39-43
If I press RIGHT twice:
- Left square: cols 14-18 -> 19-23 -> 24-28
- Right square: cols 39-43 -> 34-38 -> 29-33
After 2 RIGHTs: left at cols 24-28, right at cols 29-33. They're adjacent!
If I press RIGHT a third time:
- Left square: cols 24-28 -> 29-33
- Right square: cols 29-33 -> 24-28
They would swap columns!
But the left square is at rows 39-43, and the right square is at rows 44-48. They're at different rows.
Let me think about what happens if I bring them to the same row first. Currently:
- Left square: rows 39-43
- Right square: rows 44-48
If I press DOWN, both squares move down:
- Left square: rows 39-43 -> 44-48
- Right square: rows 44-48 -> 49-53
After 1 DOWN: left at rows 44-48, right at rows 49-53.
If I press DOWN again:
- Left square: rows 44-48 -> 49-53
- Right square: rows 49-53 -> 54-58
After 2 DOWNs: left at rows 49-53, right at rows 54-58.
So both squares move down together. To bring them to the same row, I need to:
1. Move the left square down to rows 44-48 (1 DOWN).
2. Then the left square is at rows 44-48, same as the right square's current position.
Wait, but after 1 DOWN:
- Left square: rows 44-48
- Right square: rows 49-53
They're still at different rows. The left square moved from 39-43 to 44-48, and the right square moved from 44-48 to 49-53.
Actually, looking at the transitions more carefully:
- Transition 11: DOWN - left: (39,43) -> (44,48), right: (44,48) -> (49,53)
- Transition 26: DOWN - left: (49,53) -> (54,58), right: (54,58) -> (54,58) (right blocked)
So when both squares are at different rows, they both move down. But if one is blocked, it stays.
Let me think about the goal differently. Maybe the goal is to have both squares at the same position (same row and column). Or maybe the goal is to have them overlap.
Actually, let me look at the transition where the right square moved from (44,48,39,43) to (44,48,34,38) on transition 33 (RIGHT). This means the right square moved LEFT when I pressed RIGHT. And the left square stayed at (39,43,19,23), which means it tried to move RIGHT but was blocked.
Wait, let me re-read transition 33:
- Before: left at (39,43,19,23), right at (44,48,39,43)
- After: left at (39,43,19,23), right at (44,48,34,38)
So the left square stayed at cols 19-23 (blocked from moving RIGHT), and the right square moved from cols 39-43 to cols 34-38 (moved LEFT).
This confirms that RIGHT moves the left square RIGHT and the right square LEFT. But the left square was blocked from moving RIGHT.
Now, let me think about what the goal might be. Given the mechanics:
- UP/DOWN move both squares vertically.
- LEFT/RIGHT move the squares horizontally in opposite directions.
The goal might be to:
1. Bring both squares to the center (cols 29-33) at the same row.
2. Have the squares collide or overlap.
3. Move the squares to specific positions that trigger a level completion.
Let me try a different approach. Let me check if there's a specific configuration that completes the level. Maybe I need to bring both squares to the same row and column, or to adjacent positions.
Actually, looking at the current state:
- Left square: rows 39-43, cols 14-18
- Right square: rows 44-48, cols 39-43
If I press RIGHT twice:
- Left square: cols 14-18 -> 19-23 -> 24-28
- Right square: cols 39-43 -> 34-38 -> 29-33
After 2 RIGHTs: left at (39,43,24,28), right at (44,48,29,33).
Then if I press DOWN once:
- Left square: rows 39-43 -> 44-48
- Right square: rows 44-48 -> 49-53
After 1 DOWN: left at (44,48,24,28), right at (49,53,29,33).
Then if I press UP once:
- Left square: rows 44-48 -> 39-43
- Right square: rows 49-53 -> 44-48
After 1 UP: left at (39,43,24,28), right at (44,48,29,33).
Hmm, this doesn't seem to be getting me closer to a goal.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to have both squares at the same row and column. To do that:
1. Bring both squares to the same row using UP/DOWN.
2. Bring both squares to the same column using LEFT/RIGHT.
Currently:
- Left square: rows 39-43, cols 14-18
- Right square: rows 44-48, cols 39-43
To bring them to the same row, I need to move the left square down or the right square up. But UP/DOWN moves both squares together, so I can't move them independently.
Wait, but looking at transition 6:
- Before: left at (44,48,19,23), right at (44,48,39,43)
- After: left at (39,43,19,23), right at (44,48,39,43)
So when both squares are at the same row (44-48), pressing UP moves the left square to 39-43 but the right square stays at 44-48. This means the right square was blocked from moving UP!
So the squares can be blocked independently. This means I can move them to different rows by exploiting blocking.
Let me think about the goal again. Maybe the goal is to have both squares at the same position. To do that:
1. Bring both squares to the same row.
2. Bring both squares to the same column.
Currently:
- Left square: rows 39-43, cols 14-18
- Right square: rows 44-48, cols 39-43
To bring them to the same column, I need to use RIGHT to move them toward each other:
- RIGHT: left cols 14-18 -> 19-23, right cols 39-43 -> 34-38
- RIGHT: left cols 19-23 -> 24-28, right cols 34-38 -> 29-33
- RIGHT: left cols 24-28 -> 29-33, right cols 29-33 -> 24-28
After 3 RIGHTs: left at cols 29-33, right at cols 24-28. They've crossed!
So after 2 RIGHTs: left at cols 24-28, right at cols 29-33. They're adjacent!
After 3 RIGHTs: left at cols 29-33, right at cols 24-28. They've swapped!
Hmm, but they're at different rows, so they can't collide.
Let me try a different approach. Let me just try pressing RIGHT twice and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try pressing RIGHT twice to move squares toward each other
# Current: left at (39,43,14,18), right at (44,48,39,43)
# After RIGHT x2: left at (39,43,24,28), right at (44,48,29,33)
# They would be adjacent horizontally!
result = action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
print("Result:", result)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
